pattern: Finishing all courses is possible if and only if the prerequisite graph has no directed cycle; detect cycles with DFS state-coloring (white/gray/black) or by checking whether a topological sort (Kahn's BFS) can process all nodes.
hints: Model the problem as a directed graph where an edge a → b means 'take b before a'; returning true requires that this graph is acyclic (a DAG).
DFS with three states per node — unvisited, currently in the recursion stack, fully processed — detects a cycle whenever a back-edge to a gray node is found.
Alternatively, count in-degrees and use a queue: repeatedly remove zero-in-degree nodes and reduce their neighbors' in-degrees; if all nodes are eventually removed, no cycle exists.

constraints.note: each inner pair [a,b] with 0<=a,b<numCourses; all pairs unique

edge_inputs.description: one course, no prerequisites — trivially completable
two courses, one prerequisite, no cycle — completable
two courses, mutual prerequisite — direct cycle, impossible
no prerequisites at all — all courses independent
cycle involving three nodes in a ring — not immediately obvious from the pair list
cycle buried deep in a mostly DAG graph — only one back-edge creates a cycle

stress.description: large DAG with a linear chain of 500 courses plus isolated nodes
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, size)
    # Chain 0->1->2->...->n-1 guarantees a DAG
    prereqs = [[i+1, i] for i in range(n - 1)]
    # Shuffle to avoid trivial ordering
    rng.shuffle(prereqs)
    return [n, prereqs]
stress.note: always a valid DAG — result is always True
